content.full_text:
State of California                    CONTRIBUTION U. LIFT    DISTINCTION                SOLI'D
                                                                                                    [?]

Memorandum

To  :  Opinion Request Meeting Participants              Date  : March 10, 1977

                                                                                    LI879
                                                                                    M-77-489

From  :  FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
         Ken Finney

Subject:  Item for discussion.

         A public official is invited to speak at an event and the
         sponsors of the event pay his expenses in connection with
         attendance (i.e., airfare and lodging for one evening).
         A question has arisen with respect to whether this type
         of activity should be characterized as Chapter 4 or
         Chapter 7 activity, and I would like to discuss what
         criteria we will apply in making the determination.

         Under the foregoing circumstances if the campaign com-
         mittee of the public official paid his expenses, those
         payments would be Chapter 4 expenditures pursuant to the
         Willie Brown and Janet Gray Hayes opinions. On the other
         hand, pursuant to regulation 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section
         18728, the payment of the expenses by the sponsors is
         treated as a Chapter 7 payment. This seems somewhat
         anomalous since the activity engaged in by the public
         official is precisely the same. Should the characteri-
         zation of the payments depend on the payor? Or should
         there be some criteria by which we will characterize the
         activity and the treatment of the payments will be de-
         termined by that characterization?


         KF:jo

         Once a person becomes a candidate,
         items given by the official tend to be political
         rather than personal and thus contributions rather
         than gifts. However, this presumption may be
         overcome if facts, such as proximity to the
         election and the nature of the event suggest that
         the item was personal rather than political.

                                                                    (92)

sections.question: A public official is invited to speak at an event and the sponsors of the event pay his expenses in connection with attendance (i.e., airfare and lodging for one evening). A question has arisen with respect to whether this type of activity should be characterized as Chapter 4 or Chapter 7 activity, and I would like to discuss what criteria we will apply in making the determination.
sections.question_synthetic: Should expense payments for a public official's attendance at a speaking event be characterized as Chapter 4 or Chapter 7 activity, and should this characterization depend on whether the campaign committee or event sponsors pay the expenses?
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an internal memorandum/discussion item, not an FPPC advice letter response. No formal question/conclusion sections. The final paragraph appears to be a general principle about gifts vs. contributions but is not presented as the FPPC's conclusion to the question posed. Treated as correspondence rather than an advice_letter due to its internal memo nature and lack of formal response structure.

A question has arisen with respect to whether this type of activity should be characterized as Chapter 4 or Chapter 7 activity, and I would like to discuss what criteria we will apply in making the determination.
embedding.first_500_words: State of California CONTRIBUTION U. LIFT DISTINCTION SOLI'D [?] Memorandum To : Opinion Request Meeting Participants Date : March 10, 1977 LI879 M-77-489 From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION Ken Finney Subject: Item for discussion. A public official is invited to speak at an event and the sponsors of the event pay his expenses in connection with attendance (i.e., airfare and lodging for one evening). A question has arisen with respect to whether this type of activity should be characterized as Chapter 4 or Chapter 7 activity, and I would like to discuss what criteria we will apply in making the determination. Under the foregoing circumstances if the campaign com- mittee of the public official paid his expenses, those payments would be Chapter 4 expenditures pursuant to the Willie Brown and Janet Gray Hayes opinions. On the other hand, pursuant to regulation 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18728, the payment of the expenses by the sponsors is treated as a Chapter 7 payment. This seems somewhat anomalous since the activity engaged in by the public official is precisely the same. Should the characteri- zation of the payments depend on the payor? Or should there be some criteria by which we will characterize the activity and the treatment of the payments will be de- termined by that characterization? KF:jo Once a person becomes a candidate, items given by the official tend to be political rather than personal and thus contributions rather than gifts. However, this presumption may be overcome if facts, such as proximity to the election and the nature of the event suggest that the item was personal rather than political. (92)
embedding.summary: This is an internal FPPC memorandum from Ken Finney to opinion request meeting participants raising a question about how to characterize expense payments (airfare, lodging) for a public official's attendance at a speaking event, noting an apparent inconsistency in treatment depending on whether the campaign committee or event sponsors pay.
